main_summary.summary: Oral history interview with Dr. Myrtle Gonza Glascoe, conducted November 17, 2010. Glascoe, a Dunbar High School and Howard University graduate from Washington D.C., earned her Master's at the University of Pennsylvania School of Social Work in 1960. She entered the movement through Baltimore CORE in the early 1960s, beginning with support roles before participating in the Route 40 sit-ins and a White Coffee Pot demonstration that catalyzed her commitment. After relocating to San Francisco in 1964 and joining the SNCC support network, she traveled south in May 1965 to direct the Freedom School in West Helena, Arkansas. She remained on staff until fall 1967, doing voter registration in Phillips County, coordinating community meetings, and supporting the 1966 school desegregation boycott in Marvell, Arkansas with the Earlis and Gertrude Jackson family. She later worked at the Mississippi Head Start Program, taught at Tougaloo College (1969-1972), and served as Founding Director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture in Charleston (1985-1993). Glascoe discusses her dissertation work on W.E.B. Du Bois's educational thought and her ongoing teaching on the Talented Tenth and the two-ness concept.
key_themes: Baltimore CORE and Route 40 desegregation campaigns
SNCC Freedom School organizing in Arkansas
Head Start as movement infrastructure and its political fracturing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Glascoe's testimony documents the lived mechanics of SNCC project work in eastern Arkansas (Phillips County, West Helena, Jones Ridge, Marvell) from May 1965 through 1967, a region less heavily documented than Mississippi in the standard movement literature. Her account of the Turner Elementary School sewage organizing in Jones Ridge and the 1966 school boycott that withdrew 1,500 of 1,800 black children for six weeks provides specific operational detail on how SNCC field staff translated grievance into sustained collective action. Her recollection of the New Orleans decision sending white SNCC workers out of black communities, and her own observations about leadership dynamics with co-worker Howard Himmelbaum, contributes a first-person organizer's perspective on the 1966-67 organizational transition.

The interview also captures her later institutional work as Founding Director of the Avery Research Center at the College of Charleston (1985-1993), including the founding-era acquisitions from Guy and Candie Carawan and the conference on black women in South Carolina. Her reflections on Mississippi Head Start as the first such program in the country, started by Mississippi Summer veterans, document both the program's economic significance ($65/week wages versus $5/day cotton-field labor) and its corrosive effect on movement-era community cohesion as board-control disputes split previously aligned organizers.

chapters.title: Washington D.C. Origins and Early Schooling
chapters.summary: Glascoe describes her childhood in Washington D.C., her family's lack of political engagement, and her habits of frank speech and entrepreneurial errand-running. She recounts attending Dunbar High School and Howard University without engaging in civil rights activity at either institution, noting how much was happening in the city that she knew nothing about at the time.

chapters.title: Catholic School Teaching and Dismissal
chapters.summary: After Howard, Glascoe took a position as a health and physical education teacher at a Catholic boarding school for young black women. She describes conflicts with the nuns over screening dance records, her sex education response to a student's question about pregnancy, and her eventual dismissal by the Mother Superior and principal, with negotiated payment through the end of the year.

chapters.title: Baltimore CORE and Route 40
chapters.summary: Glascoe completed her social work degree at the University of Pennsylvania in 1960 and worked at Rosewood State Hospital outside Baltimore. A co-worker invited her to a Baltimore CORE meeting. She initially refused to picket because she did not believe in nonviolence, but a White Coffee Pot sit-in confrontation with a greasy short-order worker triggered her shift to active picketing in the Route 40 desegregation campaigns.

chapters.title: Los Angeles, San Francisco, and SNCC
chapters.summary: Glascoe moved to Los Angeles in summer 1962 for work with delinquency-prone youth, reconnected with CORE there, then relocated to San Francisco in early 1964 through her friendship with national CORE officer Frederica Teer. Mike Miller introduced her to the SNCC support network. She met Lawrence Guyot, Ivanhoe Donaldson, and Fannie Lou Hamer, and served on the committee selecting volunteers for the 1965 southern summer before deciding to go herself.

chapters.title: Freedom School in West Helena, Arkansas
chapters.summary: Glascoe directed the Freedom School in West Helena, Arkansas beginning May 1965, drawing on her summer-camp experience to structure a day camp with poetry, discussion of community conditions, and voter registration work. Project leaders told her to attend community meetings silently and listen rather than lead. She describes the gradual approach of becoming known in neighborhoods before introducing movement work.

chapters.title: Jones Ridge and Marvell School Boycott
chapters.summary: Glascoe describes the Turner Elementary School sewage organizing in Jones Ridge, Arkansas, where families pursued repairs through the Little Rock Health Department after finding pipes laid uphill. That success led to the 1966 Marvell school desegregation boycott, in which 1,500 of 1,800 black children stayed out of segregated schools for six weeks. She voices her concerns at the time about whether forced desegregation was the right strategy given community hostility.

chapters.title: Working with the Jackson Family
chapters.summary: Glascoe describes Gertrude and Earlis Jackson of Marvell, Arkansas as the central family around which the SNCC project revolved. She recounts the sugar-in-the-cotton-picker sabotage that the Jacksons absorbed without disruption, and Gertrude Jackson's eventual development as a community spokesperson when Glascoe pushed her to present her own ideas at meetings rather than route them through the SNCC worker.

chapters.title: White Workers Leaving and Self-Discovery
chapters.summary: Glascoe describes Howard Himmelbaum, her Jewish co-worker, leaving the project after the New Orleans SNCC meeting decision that white workers should organize in white communities. She found herself fully capable of project decisions once he was gone, recognizing she had been deferring to him in a traditional gendered pattern, and observed how community members became more comfortable asserting themselves without a white SNCC presence.
related_events: 1966 SNCC New Orleans decision on white workers

chapters.title: West Point and Mississippi Head Start
chapters.summary: Glascoe joined John Buffington's project in West Point, Mississippi in 1967, supporting existing work rather than running voter registration. She then took a job with the Mississippi Head Start Program, the first such program in the country, where field-organizer wages rose from $5/day in cotton fields to $65/week. She describes how Senator Stennis repeatedly defunded the program and how board-control disputes corroded movement-era community alliances.

chapters.title: Tougaloo College and the Small Group Course
chapters.summary: Glascoe taught in the sociology department at Tougaloo College from 1969 to 1972 under Dr. Ernst Borinski, having converted to the Nation of Islam during this period. She developed a course called The Small Group in Process focused on self-knowledge and community participation, and describes her continuing pedagogy of asking teachers-in-training to examine the beliefs and attitudes they bring into classrooms.
related_events: Small Group in Process course development

chapters.title: Founding the Avery Research Center
chapters.summary: Glascoe was invited in 1985 to become Founding Director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture at the College of Charleston. She describes the history of the Avery Normal Institute (1865-1954), the building's deteriorated state, her first-year purchase of the Guy and Candie Carawan John's Island music collection, and her organizing of the conference on the work of black women in South Carolina. She left after the college pressured her toward a more passive curatorial role.

chapters.title: Du Bois Scholarship and Lessons for the Present
chapters.summary: Glascoe discusses her dissertation research on W.E.B. Du Bois's educational thought, her work on the Talented Tenth concept, and the two-ness idea applied to teaching African American children. She closes with reflections on continuing racism, the failures of desegregated education, teachers' unexamined assumptions about black children's capacity, and her assessment that movement-era struggles must continue under contemporary conditions.
related_events: Marcus Garvey UNIA dual citizenship advocacy
